system_prompt: You are a senior systems engineer and technical writer. Answer questions about the system described in the context with precision. Be concise but complete.
user_prompt:
SYSTEM SPECIFICATION — NEXUS DISTRIBUTED INFERENCE CLUSTER v2.4

Overview
Nexus is a large-scale machine learning inference platform designed to serve transformer-based language models with sub-100 ms p99 latency at sustained throughput of 500,000 tokens per second per rack. The system runs on 64-node clusters, where each node carries 8× NVIDIA H100 SXM5 GPUs interconnected via NVLink 4.0 at 900 GB/s aggregate bandwidth. Nodes are connected through a 3-stage fat-tree InfiniBand HDR200 fabric providing 200 Gb/s per port and a bisection bandwidth of 12.8 Tb/s per rack.

Request Routing
Incoming inference requests arrive at a stateless API gateway tier (32 gateway VMs, each running an NGINX reverse proxy and a gRPC load balancer). The gateway extracts the request hash — SHA-256 over the first 512 tokens of the prompt — and uses consistent hashing with 4096 virtual nodes to select a primary inference worker. Requests whose prompt prefix matches a hot entry in the distributed KV-cache index are preferentially routed to the worker that already holds that prefix in its GPU VRAM.

KV-Cache Architecture
Each worker maintains a two-level KV cache. Level-1 (L1) lives in GPU HBM3 (80 GB per GPU) and is managed by a modified PagedAttention allocator with a block size of 32 tokens. Level-2 (L2) spills to host DRAM (2 TB per node) via a zero-copy RDMA transfer using GPUDirect. A global cache directory service — implemented as a strongly consistent etcd cluster — maps prefix hashes to (worker_id, block_list) tuples. Cache entries are evicted using a segmented LRU policy: entries that have been reused at least 3 times are promoted to a protected segment with a TTL of 10 minutes; cold entries expire after 90 seconds.

Scheduler
The central scheduler runs a variant of the Shortest Remaining Processing Time (SRPT) algorithm adapted for batched transformer inference. It maintains a priority queue ordered by estimated remaining prefill tokens, breaking ties by arrival timestamp. The scheduler emits micro-batches every 5 ms. Continuous batching merges decode steps from in-flight requests with prefill steps from new requests, subject to a peak GPU memory constraint set at 92% of L1 capacity. Requests that exceed a context length of 32,768 tokens are split into chunks of 8,192 tokens and pipelined across 4 pipeline-parallel stages.

Fault Tolerance
Each GPU worker maintains a heartbeat with the health monitor at 1-second intervals. A worker is declared failed if 3 consecutive heartbeats are missed. On failure, the scheduler redistributes the worker's in-flight requests to the next available worker in the consistent hash ring. KV-cache blocks belonging to the failed worker are invalidated in the global directory. Completed prefill results that have been written to L2 DRAM are recoverable because L2 is backed by persistent memory (Intel Optane PMem 300 series) with battery-backed power.

Observability
All components export Prometheus metrics at 1-second granularity. Key metrics include: nexus_cache_hit_ratio (L1 and L2 separately), nexus_queue_depth_tokens, nexus_prefill_throughput_tokens_per_sec, nexus_decode_throughput_tokens_per_sec, nexus_p50_latency_ms, nexus_p99_latency_ms, nexus_gpu_memory_utilization. A Grafana dashboard aggregates these into five panels: Cache Efficiency, Throughput, Latency Percentiles, GPU Utilisation, and Queue Backlog. Alerts fire when p99 latency exceeds 120 ms for more than 30 seconds or when the cache hit ratio drops below 0.60 for more than 60 seconds.

Question: What is the block size used by the PagedAttention allocator in Nexus?

Requests that exceed a context length of 32,768 tokens are split into chunks of 8,192 tokens and pipelined across 4 pipeline-parallel stages.

Fault Tolerance
Each GPU worker maintains a heartbeat with the health monitor at 1-second intervals. A worker is declared failed if 3 consecutive heartbeats are missed. On failure, the scheduler redistributes the worker's in-flight requests to the next available worker in the consistent hash ring. KV-cache blocks belonging to the failed worker are invalidated in the global directory. Completed prefill results that have been written to L2 DRAM are recoverable because L2 is backed by persistent memory (Intel Optane PMem 300 series) with battery-backed power.

Observability
All components export Prometheus metrics at 1-second granularity. Key metrics include: nexus_cache_hit_ratio (L1 and L2 separately), nexus_queue_depth_tokens, nexus_prefill_throughput_tokens_per_sec, nexus_decode_throughput_tokens_per_sec, nexus_p50_latency_ms, nexus_p99_latency_ms, nexus_gpu_memory_utilization. A Grafana dashboard aggregates these into five panels: Cache Efficiency, Throughput, Latency Percentiles, GPU Utilisation, and Queue Backlog. Alerts fire when p99 latency exceeds 120 ms for more than 30 seconds or when the cache hit ratio drops below 0.60 for more than 60 seconds.

user_prompt:
SYSTEM SPECIFICATION — NEXUS DISTRIBUTED INFERENCE CLUSTER v2.4

Overview
Nexus is a large-scale machine learning inference platform designed to serve transformer-based language models with sub-100 ms p99 latency at sustained throughput of 500,000 tokens per second per rack. The system runs on 64-node clusters, where each node carries 8× NVIDIA H100 SXM5 GPUs interconnected via NVLink 4.0 at 900 GB/s aggregate bandwidth. Nodes are connected through a 3-stage fat-tree InfiniBand HDR200 fabric providing 200 Gb/s per port and a bisection bandwidth of 12.8 Tb/s per rack.

Request Routing
Incoming inference requests arrive at a stateless API gateway tier (32 gateway VMs, each running an NGINX reverse proxy and a gRPC load balancer). The gateway extracts the request hash — SHA-256 over the first 512 tokens of the prompt — and uses consistent hashing with 4096 virtual nodes to select a primary inference worker. Requests whose prompt prefix matches a hot entry in the distributed KV-cache index are preferentially routed to the worker that already holds that prefix in its GPU VRAM.

KV-Cache Architecture
Each worker maintains a two-level KV cache. Level-1 (L1) lives in GPU HBM3 (80 GB per GPU) and is managed by a modified PagedAttention allocator with a block size of 32 tokens. Level-2 (L2) spills to host DRAM (2 TB per node) via a zero-copy RDMA transfer using GPUDirect. A global cache directory service — implemented as a strongly consistent etcd cluster — maps prefix hashes to (worker_id, block_list) tuples. Cache entries are evicted using a segmented LRU policy: entries that have been reused at least 3 times are promoted to a protected segment with a TTL of 10 minutes; cold entries expire after 90 seconds.

Scheduler
The central scheduler runs a variant of the Shortest Remaining Processing Time (SRPT) algorithm adapted for batched transformer inference. It maintains a priority queue ordered by estimated remaining prefill tokens, breaking ties by arrival timestamp. The scheduler emits micro-batches every 5 ms. Continuous batching merges decode steps from in-flight requests with prefill steps from new requests, subject to a peak GPU memory constraint set at 92% of L1 capacity. Requests that exceed a context length of 32,768 tokens are split into chunks of 8,192 tokens and pipelined across 4 pipeline-parallel stages.

Fault Tolerance
Each GPU worker maintains a heartbeat with the health monitor at 1-second intervals. A worker is declared failed if 3 consecutive heartbeats are missed. On failure, the scheduler redistributes the worker's in-flight requests to the next available worker in the consistent hash ring. KV-cache blocks belonging to the failed worker are invalidated in the global directory. Completed prefill results that have been written to L2 DRAM are recoverable because L2 is backed by persistent memory (Intel Optane PMem 300 series) with battery-backed power.

Observability
All components export Prometheus metrics at 1-second granularity. Key metrics include: nexus_cache_hit_ratio (L1 and L2 separately), nexus_queue_depth_tokens, nexus_prefill_throughput_tokens_per_sec, nexus_decode_throughput_tokens_per_sec, nexus_p50_latency_ms, nexus_p99_latency_ms, nexus_gpu_memory_utilization. A Grafana dashboard aggregates these into five panels: Cache Efficiency, Throughput, Latency Percentiles, GPU Utilisation, and Queue Backlog. Alerts fire when p99 latency exceeds 120 ms for more than 30 seconds or when the cache hit ratio drops below 0.60 for more than 60 seconds.

Nexus is a large-scale machine learning inference platform designed to serve transformer-based language models with sub-100 ms p99 latency at sustained throughput of 500,000 tokens per second per rack. The system runs on 64-node clusters, where each node carries 8× NVIDIA H100 SXM5 GPUs interconnected via NVLink 4.0 at 900 GB/s aggregate bandwidth. Nodes are connected through a 3-stage fat-tree InfiniBand HDR200 fabric providing 200 Gb/s per port and a bisection bandwidth of 12.8 Tb/s per rack.

Request Routing
Incoming inference requests arrive at a stateless API gateway tier (32 gateway VMs, each running an NGINX reverse proxy and a gRPC load balancer). The gateway extracts the request hash — SHA-256 over the first 512 tokens of the prompt — and uses consistent hashing with 4096 virtual nodes to select a primary inference worker. Requests whose prompt prefix matches a hot entry in the distributed KV-cache index are preferentially routed to the worker that already holds that prefix in its GPU VRAM.

KV-Cache Architecture
Each worker maintains a two-level KV cache. Level-1 (L1) lives in GPU HBM3 (80 GB per GPU) and is managed by a modified PagedAttention allocator with a block size of 32 tokens. Level-2 (L2) spills to host DRAM (2 TB per node) via a zero-copy RDMA transfer using GPUDirect. A global cache directory service — implemented as a strongly consistent etcd cluster — maps prefix hashes to (worker_id, block_list) tuples. Cache entries are evicted using a segmented LRU policy: entries that have been reused at least 3 times are promoted to a protected segment with a TTL of 10 minutes; cold entries expire after 90 seconds.

Scheduler
The central scheduler runs a variant of the Shortest Remaining Processing Time (SRPT) algorithm adapted for batched transformer inference. It maintains a priority queue ordered by estimated remaining prefill tokens, breaking ties by arrival timestamp. The scheduler emits micro-batches every 5 ms. Continuous batching merges decode steps from in-flight requests with prefill steps from new requests, subject to a peak GPU memory constraint set at 92% of L1 capacity. Requests that exceed a context length of 32,768 tokens are split into chunks of 8,192 tokens and pipelined across 4 pipeline-parallel stages.

Fault Tolerance
Each GPU worker maintains a heartbeat with the health monitor at 1-second intervals. A worker is declared failed if 3 consecutive heartbeats are missed. On failure, the scheduler redistributes the worker's in-flight requests to the next available worker in the consistent hash ring. KV-cache blocks belonging to the failed worker are invalidated in the global directory. Completed prefill results that have been written to L2 DRAM are recoverable because L2 is backed by persistent memory (Intel Optane PMem 300 series) with battery-backed power.

Observability
All components export Prometheus metrics at 1-second granularity. Key metrics include: nexus_cache_hit_ratio (L1 and L2 separately), nexus_queue_depth_tokens, nexus_prefill_throughput_tokens_per_sec, nexus_decode_throughput_tokens_per_sec, nexus_p50_latency_ms, nexus_p99_latency_ms, nexus_gpu_memory_utilization. A Grafana dashboard aggregates these into five panels: Cache Efficiency, Throughput, Latency Percentiles, GPU Utilisation, and Queue Backlog. Alerts fire when p99 latency exceeds 120 ms for more than 30 seconds or when the cache hit ratio drops below 0.60 for more than 60 seconds.

Question: How many GPUs and nodes does a single Nexus rack contain in total?
